name_en: The fundamental swing — the base movement from which all capoeira flows
notes: Feet shoulder-width. Step back with the right foot, arm across the face for protection. Never cross the feet. Keep the torso swaying — this is not a static position, it is where the game breathes. Beginners tend to look at their feet; keep your eyes on the space in front of you.

name_en: The blessing kick — a straight front push kick from the back leg
notes: From ginga, push the back leg forward. Strike with the sole of the foot, not the toes. The name means 'blessing' — the kick pushes away, it does not smash. Retract the leg immediately; do not leave it hanging. The upper body leans back slightly to create distance.

name_en: Half-moon compass kick — a sweeping circular kick from a crouched position
notes: Drop into negativa first. Both hands on the ground. The kicking leg sweeps in a wide arc — think compass drawing a circle. Keep the supporting leg bent. The power comes from the hip rotation, not the leg. Look at your target throughout the sweep. Beginners rush the arc; let the circle complete.

name_en: The escape — a low crouched dodge that creates distance from an incoming kick
notes: From ginga, drop low onto one leg with the other extended straight. One hand touches the ground for balance. The chest stays open, facing your partner. The extended leg is not dead weight — it is ready to sweep. This is a defensive position, not a resting position.

name_en: The roll — a grounded rotation that transitions from defense back to standing
notes: From negativa, roll over the shoulder — not the neck. Keep the body compact, chin tucked. The hands guide the rotation. The movement finishes in ginga. This is not a gymnastic roll; it is a tactical retreat-reposition. Speed comes from keeping the core tight.

name_en: The armada — a spinning back kick executed with the outside edge of the foot
notes: From ginga, pivot on the front foot and let the back leg whip around. The arms cross the chest during the spin for protection. The kick lands with the outside edge of the foot — not the heel. Keep your eyes on the target even as you spin. Spot the target early; do not spin blind.

name_en: The jaw kick — an outside-to-inside crescent kick
notes: From ginga, lift the back leg in a sweeping arc from outside to inside. The kick traces a crescent shape. Contact is made with the outside edge of the foot. The supporting leg stays slightly bent. The upper body leans away from the kick for balance. Keep the movement fluid — it is a sweeping motion, not a snap.

name_en: The cartwheel — an inverted lateral movement used to reposition
notes: Hands placed one after the other. Legs pass overhead in sequence. The movement traces a straight line — not a banana curve. Land softly. The cartwheel in capoeira is not a gymnastic display; it is a way to move across the roda without breaking the flow. Eyes stay on the partner throughout.

name_en: The hammer kick — a sharp roundhouse kick delivered with the instep
notes: Chamber the kicking leg high, pivot on the supporting foot. Snap the kick with the instep. The name means 'hammer' — the kick should be sharp and retracted quickly. The supporting leg bends slightly for stability. Do not telegraph the kick; the chamber should come from the ginga rhythm.

name_en: The monkey — a backwards-arching flip initiated from a crouch
notes: From a low crouch, plant one hand behind you and kick the legs over. The arch comes from the hips pushing upward. This is explosive, not slow. The name reflects the movement's origins — watching monkeys in the forest. Beginners should master au first. Keep the neck relaxed — never land on the head.

name_en: The lateral dodge — a side-step evasion that drops the body away from an incoming strike
notes: From ginga, step wide to one side and drop your torso. One hand protects the face, the other touches the ground for balance. The movement is a dodge, not a lunge. Weight shifts fully onto the bent leg. Return to ginga immediately — esquiva is a momentary reposition, never a pause.

name_en: Stingray's tail — a spinning low sweep targeting the opponent's legs
notes: Drop into a one-handed stance. The sweeping leg traces the floor in a full circle. The name evokes the stingray's tail sweeping across the ocean floor. The supporting arm must be strong — the body's weight rests on it. Eyes stay on the partner's feet. The sweep should be continuous; do not stop halfway through the circle.
